item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article never gets old to me ++1. The concept is simple but would really drive you curious. The cross-testing part is also quite alright and done neatly.

By the way, I think it would really be best if this be classified as Keter, since this easily 1000x bigger than 169, and it's an entire species of eldritch worms.

Also, the Foundation obviously already knows that they would comeback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Due to change made to the site-wide CSS involving superscripts, the captions on the images were weirdly spaced. One of the captions double in on itself, and was unreadable as a result. I have updated them to be subscript, which should look identical to how the captions looked before the CSS update.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly one of my favorite SCPs on the site. It just gets better and better the more I think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey Voct, the travel times to Pluto and the edge of the heliosphere are wrong. When Pluto is at its closest to the Sun, the worm would take 48 days to pass it at this speed. The time to the edge of the heliosphere would be more like 190 days.

item.title: For the benefit of translators
item.content:encoded:
"those roach wasps" are https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerald_cockroach_wasp

"that I saw on Nova" - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nova_(TV_series)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good, but it's not good enough. It's too concise for me to make anything out of it, and the pacing, for me at least, fell flat.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Love this one because it shows why everything The Foundation knows of can't always be physically contained. Though I have to agree with a lot of the other people who said it already, is there any reason why this thing can't be classed as Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It probably shouldn't be redacted at level 5 but if it is expunged it should be expunged everywhere. Expunged means they deleted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Small qualm: data is still expunged, even when you have to have O5 access? REALLY? O5 is the highest you can be, if there is anything that is under "O5 ACCESS ONLY", it better be not expunged!

Anyway I really liked the article, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, mostly because I don't want to know the implications of what happens when it's "brood" hatches. Well done, +1. (Get it? It rhymes.)

item.title: To the Admins:
item.content:encoded: Turning ownership of this page over to Voct, who has basically been this pages godfather for about three years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher Breen

As a Half-Life nerd, I found that somewhat amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mostly like this, with some of the same mild reservations voiced by others. One big thought that it left me with is that it is a poster child for a specific missing designation. A "we sure as hell can't contain it yet, but we'd really like to" designation, so to speak. This seems to exclusively focus on information containment, which is important, but the SCP is the wyrm, not the info.

item.title: Re: Stupidity.
item.content:encoded:
The addendum says 'o-5 access', but there is still data expunged in there? Either reduce it to level 4 access or take out the expungement, o-5s have access to all information

Up voted anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But "O-5" is kinda wrong, isn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I use that format when I feel like the image needs an extended caption. It just looks better IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So…nobody cares about the image description's format? Or that it says "O-5"? No? Just me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read somewhere that this was ment to be Ziz, but I dont quite see it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
682 is as big as he needs to be to survive.

SCP-682's physical body grows and changes very quickly, growing or decreasing in size at it consumes or sheds material.

While contained, he's logically smaller than 5m3. But probably not much smaller. If he would fit in a smaller room, they would reasonablly use a smaller room.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo momma so fat, she trusts that she will draw the Jordan into her mouth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Behemoth is your mom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think 682 is roughly smaller than a city bus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couldn't 682 technically count as Behemoth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love big, things, both physical and conceptual ones. This is awesome.

So we have Ziz and Leviathan. Now someone just needs to make Behemoth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, go nuts. If theres anything off I'll just revert it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good, but there's a lot of awkward tone and incorrect grammar. With the author's permission, I'll comb through it and get everything sorted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was again swayed towards a revision to the version with the addendum. If the admins want to, you may prevent me from changing this ever again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand, it needs to be bigger. Revisions are in order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't often comment on these things, in fact I only come around periodically, but I have to say this SCP doesn't… bother me. Granted a stellar inhabitant lifeform is an intriguing idea, but given how stars work it doesn't unsettle me. The average density of our sun's material is 1.4 metric tons per cubic meter (1.408*10^3 kg/m^3). Jupiter is has a volume of about 1.4313*10^24 cubic meters. An entity of this size, at an eyeballing, would have about 2.0152704*10^25 metric tons of mass. Earth has 5.9736*10^21 metric tons of mass. This thing has four decimal places between its own mass and that of the entire Earth. If you want to know what would happen if it ever turned its attention on human beings, imagine being a fish in Mityushika Bay the day Tsar Bomba went off… and as big as that might seem, it's nowhere near as energetic as real stellar phenomena. So in short it's too powerful to induce meaningful human suffering, yet not powerful enough to challenge the laws of physics in some sanity-affecting manner.

It's a cool idea. It's a really, really cool idea. It's just not a scary one.

Also: Someone check that math, I freehanded mine, so I could be off. Given the orders of magnitude involved, way off.

item.title: Precision wonderment neutralization.
item.content:encoded: I'm not asking for people to revote, but if you're curious about the old addendum, the explanation is viewable by going into the History panel and clicking on the 15th revision. That is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reference to 028 is gone.

This makes me sad. I'm going to no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that the O-5 addon has been removed, which is a damn shame because I think by removing it it removed the meat from the SCP.
In light of this, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I liked Document 1682-N-A.

item.title: Subtle allusions
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed "disinformation protocol 1029-Wanambi".

Wanamibi, the Giant Snake from the mythology of the Pitjantjatjara people. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a nifty idea, kind of C'tan Warhammer 40,000. I'm not gonna give it an upvote because star-god threats make all other threats seem redundant, you know? Who cares if a spoon can make you eat you own eyeballs, there's a worm eating our sun!

But it's too well written and too interesting an idea to downvote so I'll keep it neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this would be substantially better without Addendum: Document 1682-N-A.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really see the threat being in the future as an issue…
The thing eating suns is pretty damned inimical to human life, even if it takes a while.
So, far more dangerous than most Keter!

Either way, I like your c'tan, so totally upvoted. >.>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no response to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wouldn't that be 2.46627566 times worse?

item.title: Re: Another one
item.content:encoded: I'm not picturing these creatures with normal delicates.

item.title: Another one
item.content:encoded: If it lays eggs, doesn't that mean there has to be another one to fertilize the eggs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After much stubborn I have decided to… well I can't change the class yet.

Euclid or Keter? The threat isn't within the foreseeable future, it's maybe hundreds upon hundreds of years ahead.

Wat do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I realized why I have such a problem with the 028 thing: it's not neutralized anymore. Before it was just a one-off thing, an interstellar traveler with better things to do than hang around us. But now we know that it's going to come back in 70-odd years to check up on it's babies.

Also, the crushing inevitability of destruction by solar tapeworm babies is less ominous and way more depressing than the sun going out a few thousand years early. I demand you remove it for the sake of my ennui!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hard to argue with that logic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bleh… just woke up. Evening. I re-read that and apparently the world is a very mean place to my subconscious 1 hour into consciousness.

Well I did do that initially, but, I wanted to tell MORE.

And thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't I just say you got extra points for it? :P That was me saying I thought it worked fine. (Though, for the record, I don't think that's automatically a good reason to do that. I'm sure you could be creative enough to forego the use of complete knowledge and get across the idea anyway…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But moose, that's how they figured out about the thingy, that SCP. There's no other way I could post it coherently if someone didn't have complete knowledge… >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fairly good, though the concept strikes as sort of… "eh" (in the "I sort of have to snicker when I think about it, and not in the good way). The execution makes it good enough for an upvote. Extra points for a cross-test that actually makes fucking sense in context and isn't designed to prop up the article by making a kewl reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And who says it's limited to c ? Or to normal space?

Plus, the frequency of its visits has to match Halley's Comet (albeit slightly out of phase).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It accelerates according the the information in one of the addendums.

Also it's a mega-titanic fire dragon thing living in the sun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Make that a second big problem. It seems like it would have no forms of propulsion in space, but to return every ~76 years, it would have to do about 10% light speed to go to and from the nearest star in that timescale.
Even travelling at c, that still limits is to only a handful of the closest stars.

I do still like the idea of it, but you're going to have to either up the velocity or give it a much longer timescale between leaving and returning.
Upping the velocity gives time dilation on it, too, which means it would live longer, so it would get more travel cycles in it's life.

item.title: Re: Identification
item.content:encoded: Yes, so many things right with this SCP, one of my new favorites.

item.title: Re: Stupidity.
item.content:encoded: I think I know what you're talking about… I'll give it a once over before the weeks-end.

item.title: Re: Stupidity.
item.content:encoded: Agreed with the second point. As for the first.. ugh, I didn't notice, but then it's not what I'd expect from a section title.

item.title: Re: Stupidity.
item.content:encoded:
The interview is labeled "SCP-028 Post-Testing Interview" so it's more or less clear what is going on. But, yeah, some kind of foreword wouldn't hurt. Especially if it would point out explicitly that the interview was conducted before 1682 was even discovered.

Terminating the subject and then putting them into 318 seems to me like a clever move, in-character. If we just suspend termination, he will die sooner or later, and not necessary in controlled environment.

item.title: Re: Stupidity.
item.content:encoded:
I still have a problem with this whole thing.
Firstly , and mainly, the article itself says nothing at all who is D-6893. This would be an example of a possibly good crossing of articles - actually fucking saying that the D-class began talking about solar worms after SCP-028 testing , and this was confirmed by observation.
Before I read about it in the comments, my reaction, which would be the in-universe reaction of anyone reading the report - Who the hell is D-6893 and why the hell should we listen to a word of what he says about the creature. Interviews have a foreword for a reason.

Secondly… it seems like terminating this sort of D-Class would be somewhat counterproductive. I mean.. we terminate them for a purpose, not just because it's a policy. Terminations have been suspended for less useful ones. But then, this is just a minor thing and i'll upvote even if it stays that way, provided something is done about point one above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like a tapeworm, behaves kind of like a roach wasp. Sun's not alive in this article. I rarely do a literal title...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which is a terrifying concept to me so I at least think it works because of that not in spite of it.

On second thought, I'm fine with that. However, I that the interviewer should have seen the implication that stars are alive, and if he was going to balk at something, he should have balked at that.

item.title: Re: Identification
item.content:encoded: Yes.
